methods.description: This method involves measuring various physical and chemical parameters of water samples, including solar irradiance, salinity, temperature, pH, dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC), and nutrients. These measurements are essential for understanding the environmental conditions and chemical composition of the water samples.

steps.description: The intensity of solar irradiance was measured with a realtime solar irradiance monitoring device (EKO, Japan).
steps.searchable_summary: measure solar irradiance using EKO device

steps.description: Salinity and temperature were measured using a salinometer and digital thermometer, respectively.
The pH was determined instantly using a pH meter (Orion Star A211, Thermo Scientific), which was calibrated against NIST-traceable pH buffers.
steps.searchable_summary: measure pH using Orion Star A211

steps.description: Samples for dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) measurements were poisoned with final concentration of 1 saturated HgCl2 solution and stored in 40 mL borosilicate glass vials until further analysis.
The DIC was determined by acidifying 0.5 mL samples and then measuring CO2 concentration using a DIC analyzer (AS-C3, Apollo, United States).
The other parameters of carbonate system were calculated from DIC and pH NBS using CO2SYS program (Lewis and Wallace, 1998) based on known levels of nutrients, temperature, and salinity.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate carbonate system parameters using CO2SYS

steps.description: Samples for nutrient measurements were filtered through 0.45 mm cellulose acetate membrane by suction filter.

The filtrate was divided into 2 of 125 mL high density polyethylene bottles: one was stored at −20°C and measured concentrations of NO3−, NO2−, PO43−, and SiO32− using an auto-analyzer (AA3, Seal, Germany) at room temperature.
Another added with 1 chloroform was stored at 4°C and measured concentration of NH4+ with indophenol blue spectrophotometry (Tri-223, Spectrum, China) at 25°C.
steps.searchable_summary: measure NH4+ concentration using indophenol blue spectrophotometry

methods.method_name: Measurements of Primary Productivity and Respiratory Carbon Loss
methods.description: This method measures primary productivity and respiratory carbon loss in water samples using radiolabeled carbon. It involves incubating samples with NaH14CO3, filtering, and measuring radioactivity to determine carbon fixation and loss.

steps.description: Water samples of 120 mL were collected and transferred immediately into 6 glass scintillation vials (3 vials were used for 12 h inoculation and 3 vials were used for 24 h inoculation) before sunrise.
steps.searchable_summary: collect and prepare water samples for incubation

steps.description: The samples were inoculated with 100 µL of 5 µCi (0.185 MBq) NaH14CO3 solution (ICN Radiochemicals, United States) for 12 h and 24 h with similar levels of sunlight and temperature as in the mesocosms.
After 12 and 24 h incubation, the cells were filtered onto glass-fiber filters (25 mm, Whatman GF/F, United States), and the filters were stored at −20°C.

Later on, the filters were thawed and exposed to HCl fumes overnight and dried at 60°C for 6 h to remove unincorporated labeled carbon, then 3 ml of scintillation cocktail (Hisafe 3, Perkin-Elmer) was added.
The incorporated radioactivity was measured by liquid scintillation counting (LS 6500, Beckman Coulter, United States).
steps.searchable_summary: measure radioactivity using liquid scintillation counting

steps.description: The respiratory carbon loss during night was calculated as the difference between carbon fixation of 12 h (day-time primary productivity) and 24 h (daily net primary productivity).

methods.description: This method measures the biogenic silica content in water samples using molybdenum-blue-ness spectrophotometry. It involves collecting samples, treating them with reagents, and measuring absorbance to determine silica content.

steps.description: Water samples of 150-500 mL for biogenic silica measurement were collected onto 2 µm polycarbonate membranes, and rinsed with filtered seawater.
steps.searchable_summary: collect and rinse samples for silica measurement

steps.description: Each membrane sample was put into 15 mL centrifuge tube, dried and stored in dryers until analysis in the laboratory.
The biogenic silica contents were measured by molybdenum-blue-ness spectrophotometry (Brzezinski and Nelson, 1995).
Briefly, the samples were placed in a boiling water bath for 40 min, after mixed with 4 mL of 0.2 mol L−1 NaOH.
steps.searchable_summary: treat samples with NaOH in boiling water bath

steps.description: Then the samples were cooled on ice, and neutralized with 1 mL of 1 mol L−1 HCl.
The supernatant, obtained by centrifuging samples at 6000 × g for 10 min, were diluted with MilliQ water, and then added with 2 mL of 0.006 mol L−1 ammonium molybdate and stood for 10 min.
steps.searchable_summary: centrifuge and treat supernatant with ammonium molybdate

steps.description: Finally, the samples were chromogenically reacted with reducing reagent for 3 h.
The absorbance of samples was measured at 810 nm using a UV-VIS Spectrophotometer (DU800, Beckman, United States).
For the calculation of biogenic silica contents, 0-30 µmol L−1 Na2SiF6 were used as standards.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate silica content using Na2SiF6 standards
